goal_09.config.title: Expanding access to the Internet
goal_09.config.abstract: Internet access has grown at remarkable speed, yet digital opportunity remains deeply unequal. While networks now reach much of the world, millions of people, especially in poorer countries, are still shut out of the digital economy. The reasons go beyond infrastructure, and reflect gaps in affordability, skills, and inclusive policy design.
goal_09.content.#0.text: Mind the gap: the global connection deficit
goal_09.content.#1.text: Access to the internet enables individuals and communities to participate in the digital economy and access vital information. By lowering the cost of economic and social transactions, it promotes innovation, efficiency, inclusion, trade, and job creation, while making workers more productive. As such, internet access is a cornerstone of development. However, without complementary policies, such as pro‑competition regulation, investments in skills and education, and accountable institutions, digital adoption may fail to deliver broad-based benefits and could even worsen inequality.[reference: int1]
goal_09.content.#2.text: How Many People Are Still Offline?
goal_09.content.#3.text: Although internet access has expanded quickly, [emphasis: over a quarter of the world’s population remains unconnected].
goal_09.content.#4.label: people are still offline
goal_09.content.#5.text: The share of people using the internet is measured primarily through household surveys: individuals are asked whether they used the Internet in the previous three months, from any device (computer, phone, TV, etc.) and any location (home, work, school, café).[footnote: If no recent survey exists, statistical modelling is used to estimate values based on correlates like mobile-subscriptions, GDP per capita, education, and regional patterns.] [reference: intm1]
goal_09.content.#6.title: Global Internet access: trends and divides
goal_09.content.#6.source: Retrieved from World Development Indicators (IT.NET.USER.ZS) and the International Telecommunications Union (ITU)
goal_09.content.#6.keyFactLabel: of people in Sub-Saharan Africa use the internet
goal_09.content.#6.scenes.#0.text: Worldwide, the share of people who access the internet has increased from 16 percent only two decades ago to over 70 percent today. Still, over a quarter of the global population remains offline.
goal_09.content.#6.scenes.#1.text: Internet adoption mirrors a country’s level of development: nearly universal in high-income nations (94 percent) but less than a quarter (23 percent) in low-income countries.
goal_09.content.#6.scenes.#2.text: Basic internet access is lowest in Sub-Saharan Africa, where only around a third (36 percent) of the population is online.
goal_09.content.#7.text: Who is left behind? Women and rural areas
goal_09.content.#8.text: Rural areas, in both high and low income economies, often lag behind urban centers when it comes to internet access, reflecting gaps in infrastructure and income.[emphasis: In rural settings, people are less likely to have broadband internet access and to use online services], such as ordering food and groceries, streaming media, or using videoconferencing for work and medical care.[reference: rur1] This disparity is especially pronounced in the rural areas of the poorest countries, where only 14% of people use the internet; even in lower middle income countries around a half of the people in rural areas are without internet access.
goal_09.content.#9.title: The urban-rural digital divide
goal_09.content.#9.subtitle: Individuals using the Internet (2025)
goal_09.content.#10.text: Another critical divide when it comes to internet access is the [emphasis: gender gap]. Women and girls are less likely to use the internet regularly, especially in low-income economies where about 30 percent of men but less than 20 percent of women are online.
goal_09.content.#12.text: There were [emphasis: 280 million more men than women using the Internet ]in 2025.[reference: gnd1]
goal_09.content.#13.text: Women, especially in low-income and rural contexts, face greater barriers to digital access compared to men, often due to affordability barriers and cultural norms.[reference: gnd2] These gender disparities exclude women from many economic, educational, and social opportunities offered by digital technologies.
goal_09.content.#14.title: Gender gaps in internet access are widest in low-income economies
goal_09.content.#14.subtitle: Individuals using the Internet (% of population), (male and female), 2025
goal_09.content.#14.source: Retrieved from World Development Indicators (IT.NET.USER.MA.ZS, IT.NET.USER.FE.ZS), ITU DataHub
goal_09.content.#16.text: High levels of internet access are concentrated in rich economies. However, despite infrastructure costs, as well as affordability and digital literacy barriers, over the past decade most countries have managed to steadily increase internet access. Notably, several lower-middle-income economies have emerged as strong overperformers when it comes to expanding  access to the internet.
goal_09.content.#17.text: The chart below shows, by country, the change in internet access levels between 2015 and 2023. It also depicts, using transparent arrows, where the countries would have been, if they had followed the typical pace of progress since 2015 (see more details about how this is calculated here). This allows us to see which countries have progressed much more than would have been expected, considering their starting point.  You can explore different aspects of progress measurement through the [emphasis: progress dashboard].
goal_09.content.#18.title: Almost all countries have managed to steadily increase internet access
goal_09.content.#18.subtitle: Individuals using the Internet (% of population)
goal_09.content.#18.scenes.#0.text: By 2024, in 54 countries more than 90 percent of the population was using the internet.
goal_09.content.#18.scenes.#1.text: Countries like [emphasis: Cambodia ]and [emphasis: Kiribati ]have made much more progress by 2023 in increasing internet usage than would have been expected based on their access levels in 2015. In Cambodia, internet access levels rose from less than 7 percent to over 60 percent of the population. Kiribati also had a remarkable performance, moving from only 15 percent of the population accessing the internet in 2015 to more than 88 percent by 2023.
goal_09.content.#19.text: Fixed vs. mobile broadband: complementary pathways
goal_09.content.#20.text: Globally, measuring internet use takes into account any online activity, even those requiring minimal data, such as emailing or browsing. However, data-intensive services, like remote learning or telemedicine, demand high-speed access or [emphasis: broadband].  There are two primary types of broadband connections: fixed and mobile.
goal_09.content.#21.text: Fixed and mobile broadband differ in how they are delivered and in the trade-offs they offer for users. [emphasis: Fixed broadband] typically provides faster, more reliable connections but depends on extensive, costly infrastructure and is therefore concentrated in denser, higher-income areas. [emphasis: Mobile broadband], by contrast, is delivered wirelessly, making it cheaper to deploy and more flexible, though often with more variable performance.
goal_09.content.#22.text: The illustration below summarizes the key differences between these two technologies.
goal_09.content.#23.title: Illustration placeholder: Fixed vs. Mobile Broadband
goal_09.content.#24.text: In practice, the two technologies are complementary: fixed broadband underpins high-capacity national networks and enterprise use, while mobile broadband drives widespread personal connectivity and inclusion.
goal_09.content.#25.text: [emphasis: Fixed broadband is common in many high-income countries], [emphasis: but nearly absent in low-income economies]. Extending fixed broadband to rural, remote, or low-income areas is often commercially unviable: infrastructure works like laying fiber-optic cables underground are expensive, while revenues remain low due to sparse populations and limited purchasing power. This mismatch between high upfront investment and low returns discourages private operators without public support or cost-sharing. [reference: brdb1][emphasis: The majority of people who use the internet do so using mobile broadband, ]especially in lower-income economies, as it is cheaper and faster to deploy.
goal_09.content.#26.title: Worldwide, mobile broadband far surpasses fixed broadband adoption
goal_09.content.#26.keyFactLabel: active mobile broadband subscriptions per 100 people in high income countries
goal_09.content.#26.scenes.#0.text: Between 2015 and 2025, fixed broadband levels doubled, reaching 20 subscriptions per 100 people. Meanwhile, [emphasis: mobile internet connectivity has surged to 99 active mobile broadband subscriptions per 100 people] (in many countries, the number of mobile broadband subscriptions exceeds 100 per 100 people because individuals and businesses often maintain multiple connections, for example for smartphones, tablets, work devices, or other connected equipment).[reference: mbrs1]
goal_09.content.#26.scenes.#1.text: Fixed broadband is common in many high-income countries, but nearly absent in low-income economies. In [emphasis: Senegal] and [emphasis: Côte d’Ivoire], fixed broadband penetration is minimal: there are 2 and 1.4 fixed broadband subscriptions per 100 people
goal_09.content.#26.scenes.#2.text: [emphasis: Across much of the world, people connect to the internet through mobile phones]: in both Senegal and in Côte d’Ivoire there are 108 mobile broadband subscriptions per 100 people. Both countries have internet access levels above the regional average for Sub-Saharan Africa (34 percent): in Senegal about 60 percent and in Cote d’Ivoire about 40 percent of the population uses the internet.
goal_09.content.#28.text: Globally, most internet users use their mobile phones and mobile broadband to go online. Mobile broadband provides wireless internet access via cellular networks (mostly through 4G or 5G) using SIM-based devices (like cell phones) or fixed-wireless terminals.[emphasis: ]
goal_09.content.#29.text: [emphasis: Mobile networks have advanced rapidly over the past three decades], evolving from basic digital communication to highly sophisticated connectivity. Second-generation (2G/GSM) networks in the 1990s marked the shift from analog (similar to traditional radio broadcast, with no encryption) to digital (information converted into digital data and encrypted before transmission). This improved voice quality and enabled text messaging. Third-generation (3G) technology brought mobile internet into everyday use, making web browsing and video calls widely accessible. Fourth-generation (4G/LTE) networks have delivered broadband-like speeds, supporting high definition video and today’s data‑intensive apps. [reference: netc1]
goal_09.content.#30.title: From 1G to 5G: The Progression of Mobile Technology
goal_09.content.#31.text: [emphasis: The latest generation of mobile networks (5G) is designed to transmit data very quickly.] This makes it possible for large numbers of everyday objects, such as sensors and appliances to be connected and to communicate continuously (a concept known as the “Internet of Things”).[reference: iot1] This capability supports better public services and the development of smart cities (for example, allowing traffic lights to adjust to congestion).[reference: iot2] Other applications include the use of connected sensors and monitoring systems to detect potential equipment collisions or dangerous environmental conditions, which can greatly improve worker safety.[reference: iot3]
goal_09.content.#32.title: Most of the world is covered by 3G and 4G networks. Sub-Saharan Africa is the least connected region
goal_09.content.#32.subtitle: Percentage of inhabitants that are within range of at least a 3G, 4G, or 5G mobile-cellular signal, irrespective of whether or not they are subscribers
goal_09.content.#32.scenes.#0.text: Currently, 96% of the global population lives within reach of at least a  3G network.
goal_09.content.#32.scenes.#1.text: Although 4G networks cover over 93% of the people in the world, [emphasis: low income countries, mostly in Sub-Saharan Africa, still face stark gaps in 4G coverage], with rural areas especially underserved.
goal_09.content.#32.scenes.#2.text: 5G is widely available in many high-income economies. In some upper and lower-middle-income countries like China, India, or Thailand over 80 percent of people are also covered by 5G networks.
goal_09.content.#32.scenes.#3.text: In Sub-Saharan Africa, the deployment of 5G remains largely confined to a few high-density urban areas, and only [emphasis: Mauritius ]and [emphasis: South Africa ]have coverage rates exceeding 50 percent of the population.
goal_09.content.#33.text: [emphasis: Expanding mobile network coverage in low income countries is challenging due to high infrastructure costs and weak supporting systems.] Although not as costly as introducing fixed broadband, mobile broadband still requires significant investments. Many underserved areas lack essential infrastructure like mobile towers and backhaul (the fiber‑optic cables that connect mobile towers to the core internet network), making deployment far costlier than elsewhere. [reference: infr1] [reference: infr2] Power supply is a major bottleneck: remote sites often rely on diesel or off-grid solutions, adding significant capital and maintenance costs.[reference: infr3] Sparse rural populations drive up cost per user and reduce returns, weakening commercial incentives.
goal_09.content.#34.text: Being able to connect to the internet is the first step in taking advantage of the digital services and opportunities. However, once online, connection speed determines the type of activities people can do.
goal_09.content.#36.text: Internet speed heavily impacts the quality of online experiences. [emphasis: Fast broadband speed supports economic growth, productivity and innovation, and allows access to better education and healthcare services.] [reference: spd1] [reference: spd2] [reference: spd3] In countries like Singapore, Chile, or United Arab Emirates the median download speeds for either mobile or fixed broadband, or both, are high and supportive of a wide range of activities (over 300 Mbps), while in some low-income economies, median internet connections are often much slower (under 20 Mbps). Improving internet speed is crucial for enabling people to use advanced applications and services.
goal_09.content.#37.title: High income countries enjoy high speed internet
goal_09.content.#37.subtitle: Median country download speeds (mbps), fixed broadband
goal_09.content.#37.source: Speedtest Global Index (to add date of retrieval)
goal_09.content.#38.text: From coverage to connection: the usage gap
goal_09.content.#39.text: There is often a significant gap between network coverage and actual internet use.[emphasis:  Even when broadband networks reach most of the population, many people remain offline due to high costs for devices and data packages as well as low levels of digital literacy.] For example, in Bangladesh, 4G mobile networks covered 99% of the population, yet only 45% of people were observed to be using the internet (as of 2023). This disparity highlights the difference between network coverage and the ability to use online resources.
goal_09.content.#40.label: covered by a network are not online
goal_09.content.#41.text: This disconnect also shows that infrastructure alone is not enough to achieve true digital inclusion. Understanding the reasons why people remain unconnected, even when network coverage is available, is essential for designing effective and equitable interventions that facilitate meaningful access and use.
goal_09.content.#42.title: Mobile networks now reach most of the population, but many people remain offline
goal_09.content.#43.text: Digital literacy: the key to meaningful access
goal_09.content.#44.text: Digital literacy is essential for meaningful internet use. [emphasis: Many people lack the skills needed to navigate online resources and to communicate through mobile devices], underscoring the importance of education and training initiatives. In some countries, more than half of the population has never sent a text message using a mobile phone.[emphasis:  ]This gap in technology usage  illustrates how limited digital skills can prevent individuals from benefiting fully from available connectivity.
goal_09.content.#45.title: In 27 countries more the 50% of people have never sent a text message
goal_09.content.#45.subtitle: Share of respondents who report that they have never sent a text message through a mobile phone (SMS, text message or through a messaging app)
goal_09.content.#45.source: The Global Findex Database (2025), indicator: [con16] "Can send a text message on a mobile phone (%, age 15+)". The data shows the percentage of respondents who report that they sent a text message through a mobile phone. This can include an SMS or text message or a message on a messaging app, such as WhatsApp.
goal_09.content.#47.text: One of the biggest reasons many communities remain offline is simply the cost of devices such as smartphones and computers. The World Bank’s 2025 FINDEX report shows that not having enough money to buy a smartphone is the leading barrier to owning one, and without a smartphone people cannot use the internet. [emphasis: The device cost barrier is even more acute for adults in the poorest 40% of the population.] In some countries in Sub-Saharan Africa, the average cost of a smartphone can represent almost 40% of an adult’s monthly income [reference: devaf1], and about 53% of the monthly income in South Asia[reference: devaf2]. Improving access to affordable devices is not just helpful but essential for real digital inclusion.
goal_09.content.#48.title: Few people in low income countries have smartphones
goal_09.content.#48.source: The Global Findex Database (2025), indicators: [con9a] “Main mobile phone is a smartphone (%, age 15+)”;
goal_09.content.#48.mobile_not_main: Share of people not owning a smartphone
goal_09.content.#48.lack_of_money: Lack of money as a reason for having no smartphone
goal_09.content.#48.keyFactLabel: of people in low income countries own a smartphone
goal_09.content.#48.scenes.#0.text: [emphasis: The share of people who do not have a smartphone remains high in poorer countries], reaching nearly 80% in low-income economies and about 50% in lower-middle-income economies.
goal_09.content.#48.scenes.#1.text: In several countries, including [emphasis: in Malawi, ][emphasis: Madagascar][emphasis: , and Chad, fewer than 14% of adults own a smartphone]. These patterns highlight the strong association between income levels and smartphone ownership, and point to the persistent barriers faced by the poorest population groups in accessing more advanced mobile technologies.
goal_09.content.#49.text: Access to the internet has become a gateway to a myriad of opportunities. Being connected opens the door to better communication, access to information, and new ways of participating in the economy. When the infrastructure for connection exists, access to digital opportunities can transform lives. The countries’ achievements suggest that, with sustained effort, closing the remaining gaps between coverage and meaningful internet use is within reach.
goal_09.references.keyOfReference: Here goes the content of the reference, which can include links like this. Add a reference in the text with [reference: keyOfReference]
